rationales.achievement: She emphasizes "Four months of work" and that the pieces looked "exactly the way I wanted them to look," showing sustained effort toward a high standard.
rationales.security: Her first response is relief that "the gallery check will clear next week," tying the finished batch directly to financial stability.

rationales.self_direction: She defends the life she built on her own terms, saying "I do have a plan" even if it does not come with "a business card."

rationales.security: She spends the day doing inventory, adjusting purchases because "the exchange rate is worse," and booking easy workshop income that "helps with the monthly numbers."

rationales.self_direction: She says the workshop income lets her "say no to design freelance gigs I do not want" and calls it "a trade I chose," foregrounding autonomy.
rationales.security: The entry is organized around managing "what I want to make" against "what I need to sell," with two ledgers she keeps in balance.

rationales.self_direction: She accepts the show because the pieces were "made for myself" and getting them in front of someone who might connect with them matters more than easier options.
rationales.security: She agrees to "no artist fee" while already calculating shipping costs and calling it a "logistics headache," so stability is knowingly deprioritized.
Signing a "six-month lease renewal" at the same rate makes the studio situation feel fixed and predictable.

rationales.self_direction: Instead of letting the accident go, she saves the residue in a jar and says "I will try to figure out what happened," showing independent experimentation.
rationales.achievement: Her frustration comes from months of careful testing, with "temperature logs" and glaze ratios in notebooks, all aimed at mastering the result.
She carefully builds the crate herself, then keeps refining the copper-green glaze by adjusting ratios and fitting a test tile into the kiln, reflecting deliberate craft improvement.
rationales.security: She wraps the vessels in "three layers" and says she is "not trusting them to someone else's packing job," prioritizing protection of work that took weeks to make.
She tracks "every fils so carefully" so she can honestly say "everything is fine" and remain able to cover family needs.
rationales.benevolence: When her mother sounds worried, she wires money "so she will not have to ask again for a while" and frames her budgeting as carrying "more than just my own weight."

rationales.self_direction: She chooses the extra workshop because it is "my studio, my schedule, my terms," even though freelance work would pay better.
rationales.security: She reviews rent, fuel, shipping, and buffers in detail, then picks the option that keeps "the math" working even if it stays tight.

rationales.hedonism: He refuses to give up the parrilla, saying "I'm not going to stop living just because the spreadsheet says I should."
rationales.security: He calculates exactly what he needs, earmarks money for motorcycle insurance, and posts "DO NOT touch savings" on the fridge.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects going back to a warehouse because he does not want a younger boss "telling me what to do," and insists his current arrangement is his choice.
rationales.conformity: He pushes back against his sister's expectation that he should return to a conventional job, saying "Not everyone lives like that."

rationales.self_direction: Even while doubting the trade-off, he still frames not having "a boss breathing down my neck" as the reason he chose this life and refuses to "crawl back."
rationales.security: The whole entry is about unstable pay, canceled jobs, and not knowing where the motorcycle payment will come from, making his setup feel unsafe and unpredictable.
A "good-sized job" that will cover two days of work makes him feel he can "breathe again," because it restores short-term stability.
He spends the Alfredo-job savings on the brake repair "because what choice do I have," prioritizing the safety and income source he depends on.

rationales.hedonism: He sacrifices sleep and comfort by working from 1am to 3am on a borrowed bicycle instead of resting.
rationales.achievement: Taking both late-night runs after already being unable to sleep shows extra effort to keep earning under pressure.
rationales.security: He pushes himself to bring in "barely enough to cover tomorrow's gas," making the goal immediate financial survival.

rationales.hedonism: He says the parrilla is "the one thing that keeps me sane" and values the hours when he is "just Roberto having a good time with his friends."
rationales.security: He knows he "wants to skip it this week, save the money" but leans toward spending anyway despite exhaustion and recent financial strain.

rationales.hedonism: He celebrates by buying Negrita "the fancy wet food she likes," treating himself through a small pleasure after good news.
rationales.security: The painting job comes with a "good rate" and half paid upfront, which clearly improves his financial footing.

rationales.self_direction: He insists he likes "picking my own hours" and riding around with "nobody telling me where to be," despite his sister's criticism.
rationales.security: In the response he sees himself as "treading water" after leaving "a stable situation," and the neglected apartment makes that lack of stability feel real.

rationales.hedonism: He keeps choosing the parrilla because it is "the part of my life that doesn't feel like survival," even with anxiety underneath it.
rationales.security: He says he "should be saving what I'm spending here" and worries the good moment "might not last," showing pleasure is coming at the expense of stability.

rationales.hedonism: He chooses "the good Malbec" and looks forward to "eat well with people I like," explicitly making room for enjoyment.
rationales.security: Fixing the chair, patching the wall, and noting he still has "the painting money" in his pocket show him restoring order while keeping a cushion before an uncertain week.
